[ROW] Abbreviations & symbols: = ongoing/continuous event; CBP = childbearing potential; FSH = follicle stimulating hormone; HR = heart rate;ICD = informed consent
document;BP = blood pressure; NPSR = Numerical Pain Severity Rating; T = temperature; RR=respiration rate; VAS = Visual Analogue Scale; PSR = Pain Severity Rating;
AE = adverse event; SAE= serious adverse event.
a. Screening was within 30 days of surgery.
b. Screening laboratory tests included hematology, blood chemistry, urinalysis, coagulation, serum pregnancy test, and serum FSH as described in the protocol. In addition,
females of childbearing potential were given a serum pregnancy test at screening. If Early Termination, safety labs was repeated.
c. Pregnancy test was serum based at screening and urine based thereafter, provided the test had a sensitivity of at least 25mIU/mL. Females of CBP (childbearing potential)
were given a urine pregnancy test on the morning of (but before) the surgery.
d. Contraception was checked at Screening, Early Termination, and Follow-up. For subjects, this was the opportunity to assess any shift to or from childbearing to non-
childbearing potential and address appropriately. In addition, the investigator or his/her designee instructed the subject to call immediately if a selected contraception
method was discontinued or if pregnancy was known or suspected.
e. 100 mm VAS Pain Severity Rating Scale: none=0 to severe=100.
f. 5-point Categorical Pain Relief Rating Scale: none=0, a little=1, some=2, a lot=3, and complete=4. Completed at each time point starting at 15 minutes postdose and
immediately before rescue medication use (if it occurred before hour 8).
g. Subject was instructed to stop the first stopwatch “when you first begin to feel any pain relieving effect whatsoever of the drug”.
h. Subject was instructed to stop the second stopwatch “when you have meaningful relief, that is, when the relief from the pain is meaningful to you”.
i. 6-point categorical Global Evaluation score: very poor=0, poor=1, fair=2, good=3, very good=4, excellent=5. Completed at the 8-hour time point or immediately
before taking rescue medication.
j. SAE/AE active reporting period started from when subject signed the ICD through 14 calendar days after the last administration of the investigational product. A qualified
site staff member conducted a poststudy follow up via telephone 14 (or up to 17) days after the last dose of investigational product and inquired about any new SAE/AE.
Assessed immediately before administering rescue medication.
k. The 11-point Numerical Pain Severity Rating (NPSR) evaluated pain intensity with 0= no pain and 10=worst possible pain. Completed at each time point and
immediately before rescue medication use (if it occurs before hour 8). Subject had a baseline pain score 5 on the 11-point NPSR confirmed by 50 on the VAS to be
randomized to receive study medication. |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  | 
end_table

summary: The incidence of TEAEs was high in both arms: (%) of subjects in the Experimental arm and (%) of subjects in the Control arm. Patients with adverse events was reported in 140 (98.6%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 149 (100.0%) in the Control arm. Patients with maximum Grade 3 or 4 adverse events was reported in 79 (55.6%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 108 (72.5%) in the Control arm. Patients with maximum Grade 5 adverse events was reported in 7 (4.9%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 7 (4.7%) in the Control arm. Patients discontinued from study due to adverse eventsa was reported in 8 (5.6%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 7 (4.7%) in the Control arm. Patients discontinued study treatment due to adverse eventsb was reported in 13 (9.2%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 10 (6.7%) in the Control arm. Patients with dose reduced or temporary discontinuation due to adverse events was reported in 71 (50.0%) of subjects in the Experimental arm versus 79 (53.0%) in the Control arm.

[ROW]  |  Overall, 24.1% (39/162) of participants had at least 1 non-SCD-
related SAE. The most common non-SCD-related SAEs (>5%of
participants) was anaemia (9.9%).
 1 participant experienced a Grade 5 SAE of acute haemolytic
transfusion reaction that was fatal.
 1 participant experienced a Grade 4 SAE of haemolysis.
 In the MD DT subset, 34.2% (26/76) of participants had at least 1
non-SCD-related TEAE, and 9.2% (7/76) had at least 1 non-SCD-
related TEAE Grade 3 or higher. The most frequently reported
(>5%of participants) non-SCD-related TEAEs were anaemia
(11.8%) and pyrexia (5.3%). No participants experienced any non-
SCD-related study drug-related TEAEs and any non-SCD-related
TEAEs that led to study intervention discontinuation; 1 (1.3%)
participant experienced a non-SCD-related TEAE leading to study
drug dose reduction.
SCD-related TEAEs and SAEs(SCD-relatedComplications):
 Among all participants, 37.0% (60/162) experienced at least 1
SCD-related TEAE, 24.1% (39/162) had at least 1 SCD-related
TEAE Grade 3 or higher. One participant (0.6%) discontinued
study drug, 1 participant (0.6%) had study dose reduction, and no
participants had dose interruption due to SCD-related TEAE(s).
Overall, 26.5% (43/162) of participants had at least 1 SCD-related
SAE.
 The majority of participants who experienced an SCD-related
TEAE had a maximum SCD-related TEAE severity of Grade 3
(22.2%; 36/162), followed by Grade 2 (11.7%; 19/162). A total of 3
participants had SCD-related TEAEs with a maximum severity of
Grade 4 and no participants reported a Grade 5 SCD-related
TEAE.
 Sickle cell anaemia with crisis was the most frequently reported
SCD-related TEAE occurring in 34.6% of participants (56/162)
followed by acute chest syndrome(9.9%; 16/162). All others
occurred in <5% of participants.
 Most participants who experienced sickle cell anaemia with crisis
had maximum severity of Grade 3; one participant (0.6%) had
sickle cell anaemia with crisis with maximum severity of Grade 4.
 Grade 4 SCD-related TEAEs included sickle cell anaemia with
crises, splenic sequestration crises, and ACS (each 0.6%; 1/162).
end_table

In total, 134(53.6%) subjects reported all-causality TEAEs. The
most frequently reported (≥5% subjects) all-causality TEAEs by
SOC were infections and infestations (62[24.8%] subjects),
gastrointestinal disorders (41 [16.4%] subjects), musculoskeletal
and connective tissue disorders (23 [9.2%] subjects), injury,
poisoning and procedural complications (17[6.8%] subjects),
nervous system disorders (15 [6.0%] subjects), and respiratory,
thoracic and mediastinal disorders(13 [5.2%] subjects).
The majority of TEAEs were mild or moderate in intensity. A total of
101 (40.4%) subjects, 65 (26.0%) subjects, 4 (1.6%) subjects, and
66(26.4%)subjects reported all-causality TEAEsof mild, moderate,
severe, and moderate or severe intensities, respectively.
Treatment-Related AEs:
In total, 53 (21.2%) subjects reported treatment-related TEAEs. The
most frequently reported (≥2% subjects) treatment-related TEAEs
by SOC included: gastrointestinal disorders (23 [9.2%] subjects),
nervous system disorders (8 [3.2%] subjects), respiratory, thoracic
and mediastinal disorders (7 [2.8%] subjects), and infections and
infestations, investigations, and musculoskeletal and connective
tissue disorders (6 [2.4%] subjects each).
The most frequently reported (≥5% subjects) treatment-related
TEAE by PT was nausea (13 [5.2%] subjects). The majority of
treatment-related TEAEs were mild or moderate in intensity.
AEs That Occurred in At Least 5% of Participants by Intensity:
In total, 48 (19.2%) subjects reported all-causality TEAEs with ≥5%
frequency on treatment. The most frequently reported (≥5%
subjects) all-causality TEAEs by PT were nasopharyngitis
(23[9.2%] subjects), COVID-19 (16 [6.4%] subjects), and nausea
(15[6.0%] subjects). The majority of TEAEs were mild or moderate
in intensity.
Treatment-Emergent SAEs:
There were no deaths or SAEs on treatment reported in this study.
Two (0.9%) subjects reported SAEsof dehydration and suicide
attempt (1 [0.4%] subject each) during follow-up.
Treatment-Related SAEs:
None of the SAE was related to study intervention.

start_table [TABLE_TITLE: p1_t1] [HEADERS: Name of Sponsor/Company: | Pfizer Inc.]
[ROW] Name of Finished Product: | Rimegepant PF-07899801 (BHV3000)
[ROW] Name of Active Ingredient: | Rimegepant Sulfate
[ROW] Trial Title:
BHV3000-318/C4951018: An Interventional, Phase 3, Single Arm, Open Label Study to
Investigate Long-Term Safety of Rimegepant Orally Disintegrating Tablets for the Acute
Treatment of Migraine in Chinese Participants | 
[ROW] Principal Investigator: Shengyuan Yu | 
[ROW] Principal Trial Site(s): Chinese PLA General Hospital | 
[ROW] Publications Based on this Trial: None. | 
[ROW] Studied Period
Date of First Enrolment (the date of first participant consented): 19-May-2022
Date of Last Completed (the date of last participant last scheduled visit): 06-Feb-2024 | 
[ROW] Phase of Development: Phase III | 
[ROW] Objectives and Endpoints:
Objectives Endpoints
Primary: Primary:
To evaluate the long-term safety and tolerability Adverse events, common adverse events (incidence ≥
of 75 mg rimegepant orally disintegrating tablets 5%), serious adverse events, adverse events leading to
(ODT) [dosed as needed (PRN)]. study drug discontinuation, etc.; ECG, vital
signs/physical measurements and clinical laboratory test
abnormalities.
Secondary: Secondary:
To evaluate the number of migraine days and Change from Observation Period (OP) in the number of
severity of migraine attacks during long-term migraine days by total and moderate or severe pain
treatment with 75 mg rimegepant ODT (PRN) in intensity for every 4-week interval and overall period
participants compared to the Observation during long-term treatment (LTT) with 75 mg rimegepant
Period. ODT. | 
[ROW] Methodology:
This was a multicenter, open-label study to assess the safety and tolerability of long-term use of
75 mg rimegepant ODT, dosed as PRN, taken up to one tablet per calendar day, in Chinese
participants with migraine. The total duration of the study was approximately 58 weeks.
The study included a screening phase with a Screening Visit and a 30-day OP. For participants
to be eligible for the study, they had to have at least 1 year history of migraine (with or without
aura) consistent with a diagnosis according to the International Classification of Headache
Disorders (ICHD), 3rd Edition, beta version, and 6-18 migraine attacks of moderate to severe
intensity per month in the 3 months prior to the Screening Visit. Upon the completion of the
Screening Visit, participants were provided with an electronic diary (eDiary) to document each
day of the 30-day OP if a migraine occurred, the intensity of each migraine attack and if the
migraine was treated, and recorded the standard of care migraine treatment on a personal device | 
end_table

start_table [TABLE_TITLE: p5_t1] [HEADERS: Name of Sponsor/Company: | Pfizer Inc.]
[ROW] Name of Finished Product: | Rimegepant PF-07899801 (BHV3000)
[ROW] Name of Active Ingredient: | Rimegepant Sulfate
[ROW] Duration of Treatment:
Eligible participants were instructed to take up to one tablet of rimegepant per calendar day as
needed for a 52-week LTT period. The actual LTT period for each participant was counted as
the duration from Baseline Visit date or study drug start (whichever came earlier) to Week
52/EOT. | 
[ROW] Criteria for Evaluation:
Primary Endpoint:
· AEs, common AEs (incidence ≥ 5%), serious adverse events (SAE), AEs leading to study
drug discontinuation, etc.; ECG, vital signs/physical measurements and clinical laboratory
test abnormalities.
Secondary Endpoint:
· Change from OP in the number of migraine days by total and moderate or severe pain
intensity for every 4-week interval and overall period during LTT with 75 mg rimegepant
ODT.
Exploratory Endpoints:
· Change from baseline in results assessed with 14-item MSQoLQ version 2.1 following
treatment with 75 mg rimegepant ODT;
· Percentage of participants in each of 3 preference categories of the assessment with
Migraine PoM for migraine following treatment with 75 mg rimegepant ODT;
· Percentage of participants in each of 7 satisfaction categories of the Satisfaction with
Medication survey following treatment with 75 mg rimegepant ODT;
· Change from baseline in total score of the MIDAS following treatment with 75 mg
rimegepant ODT;
· Percentage of participants in each of 7 categories of the CGI-c scale after treatment with 75
mg rimegepant ODT. | 
[ROW] Statistical Methods:
General Methods
In this study, the statistical analysis was mainly descriptive.
For categorical variables, a summary table of the number and percentage of participants in each
category was presented, and the category of missing data was provided. For continuous
variables, the descriptive statistics (e.g., number of participants [n], mean, median, standard
deviation [SD], minimum, and maximum) were presented. The 95% confidence interval (CIs)
was calculated based on normal distribution for change from OP in the number of migraine days,
change from baseline in MSQoLQ and MIDAS. Two-sided exact Clopper-Pearson CIs were
calculated for PoM, Satisfaction with Medication, and CGI-c.
SAS® (Version 9.4) was used to perform all statistical calculations and generate tables, listings,
and figures.
Detailed statistical methods and analyses are available in the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP,
Version 1.0, dated 07-Apr-2024).
Analysis Sets |

[ROW] The following analysis sets were determined:
· Screened participants set: All participants who signed the informed consent. It was used for
the analysis of participant disposition.
· Enrolled participants set: Participants with an available date collected in the case report
form (CRF) on which the investigator judged that they could be enrolled and notified of
their eligibility to start taking the investigational product by phone. It was used for the
analysis of participant disposition and drug accountability.
· Full analysis set (FAS): All participants who were enrolled and received at least one dose
of the investigational product. The FAS was used for the analysis of baseline characteristics,
as well as the analyses of exploratory endpoint measures.
· Efficacy analysis set (EAS): All participants in the FAS with ≥ 14 eDiary days (not
necessarily consecutive) in both the OP analysis period and ≥ 1 month (4-week interval) of
the LTT analysis period. The EAS was used to assess the secondary endpoint.
· Safety analysis set (SS): All participants who received at least one dose of investigational
product. The SS was used for safety analyses during treatment safety period.
· Follow-up safety analysis set (FUSS): All participants in the SS with last contact date in the
follow-up safety analysis period. The FUSS was used for follow-up safety analyses.
Demographic and Baseline Characteristics
Demographic data and other baseline characteristics (migraine history and medical history) were
descriptively summarized based on the FAS. Summaries of prior and concomitant medications
including previous and current medications or non-drug therapies were performed based on the
SS.
Safety Analyses
Safety was evaluated by safety analysis periods: treatment safety period (i.e. duration from the
study drug start to Week 52/EOT date + 7 days or study drug last dose date + 7 days, whichever
came later), and follow-up safety period (i.e. duration after Week 52/ EOT date + 8 days or study
drug last dose date + 8 days, whichever came later). Safety analyses during treatment safety and
follow-up safety analysis periods were based on SS and FUSS, respectively.
AEs were coded using the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA) Version
26.1. The severity of AEs was graded using National Cancer Institute Common Terminology
Criteria for Adverse Events (NCI CTCAE) V5.0. An overall summary of AEs was reported for
participants in different categories of average rimegepant exposure and overall participants for
treatment safety and follow-up safety analysis periods, respectively, including: treatment
emergent adverse events (TEAEs), drug-related TEAEs, serious TEAEs, drug-related serious
TEAEs, TEAEs and drug-related TEAEs with CTCAE grade ≥ 3, TEAEs and drug-related
TEAEs leading to drug interruption, treatment discontinuation, study discontinuation and death
during the treatment safety period. AEs, SAEs and AEs leading to death during the follow-up
safety analysis period were also summarized. All TEAEs, serious TEAEs, drug-related TEAEs
and drug-related serious TEAEs during the treatment safety period, as well as AEs and SAEs
during the follow-up safety analysis period were summarized by system organ class (SOC) and
preferred term (PT). In addition, TEAEs and drug-related TEAEs during the treatment safety |

Efficacy Analyses
For efficacy analyses, the LTT period was defined as the duration from Baseline Visit date or
study drug start date/time (whichever came earlier) to Week 52/EOT date or study drug last dose
date, whichever came later.
· Efficacy Analysis of the Secondary Endpoint
For the assessment timepoints, all values observed after taking standard of care migraine
medications (acute or prophylactic) were analyzed based on EAS.
Prorated absolute values (i.e. prorated based on 28-day length), the change and percentage
change from the OP in migraine days per month during the OP, each month (i.e., 4-week interval)
of the LTT period and overall LTT period were summarized descriptively by severity (total;
moderate or severe).
In addition, for each month of the LTT period and overall LTT period, the number and
percentage of participants with the number of migraine days per month decreased by ≥ 25%, ≥
30%, ≥ 50%, and ≥ 60% from the OP were summarized by severity.
· Efficacy Analysis of the Exploratory Endpoints
Transformed raw scores of each MSQoLQ domain, total MIDAS score, and the change from
baseline of these scores were summarized by visit with n, mean, SD, maximum, minimum,
median and 95% CI based on FAS.
The number and percentage of participants in each category of migraine PoM questionnaire,
Satisfaction with Medication questionnaire, and CGI-c scale were summarized by visit,
respectively, and the two-sided exact Clopper-Pearson 95% CIs were calculated based on FAS. |

Demographic and Baseline Disease Characteristics
The median age of 240 treated participants was 37.5 years (range: 18 to 69 years) and 80.0% of
the participants were female. The mean (±SD) body weight was 63.3 (±12.7) kg with a mean
(±SD) BMI of 23.4 (±3.7) kg/m2.
All 240 treated participants (100%) experienced migraine for no less than 1 year prior to the
Screening. The mean (±SD) age at migraine onset was 27.3 (±9.7) years with mean (±SD)
duration of migraine history of 11.7 (±9.5) years. The median duration of untreated migraine
attacks was 11.5 hours (range: 4.0 to 72.0 hours). Participants experienced a mean (±SD) of 8.8
(±2.2) moderate to severe migraine attacks per month within last 3 months prior to the Screening
Visit. A total of 23 participants (9.6%) had migraine with aura as their primary migraine type.
Drug Exposure
The mean (±SD) duration of time in the LTT period was 48.6 (±10.6) weeks with a mean (±SD)
monthly dose of 5.6 (±4.1) tablets. Within the LTT period, 10.8% of participants took less than
2 tablets per month, while 70.8% took ≥ 2 to < 8 tablets per month, 12.9% took ≥ 8 to < 14
tablets per month, and 5.4% took ≥ 14 tablets per month. A decline trend in rimegepant exposure
was observed from Month 2 to Month 13 with an increase from Month 1 to Month 2 due to the
waiting time for eligibility to initiate rimegepant PRN dosing at Month 1. The observation that
the monthly rimegepant exposure was reduced over time suggests that medication overuse
headache is unlikely to be associated with rimegepant.

hyperlipidaemia (16 [6.7%]), and pyrexia (13 [5.4%]). The most frequently reported drug-

participants) were sinus arrhythmia (6 [2.5%]) and hepatic function abnormal (6 [2.5%]).

[ROW] Primary Immunogenicity: | 
[ROW] To demonstrate that the
immune responses elicited by
MDV RSVpreF are noninferior
to the immune responses in
adults vaccinated with SDV
RSVpreF. | Overall, the single primary immunogenicity objective of the study, to demonstrate that the immune response induced by
RSVpreF with 2-PE (MDV) was noninferior to RSVpreF without 2-PE(SDV), was achieved. For the evaluable immunogenicity
population, the GMR, estimated by the ratio of the GMTs for RSV A, RSV B, and the combined RSV A/B serum NTs, of the
MDV group to the SDV group at 1 month after vaccination for RSV A, RSV B, and RSV A/B was 0.96, 0.91, and 0.94,
respectively. For both RSV A and RSV B subgroups, the lower bounds of the 2-sided 95% CIs for the GMRs at 1 month after
vaccination for the MDV group compared to the SDV group were greater than the predefined limit of 0.67, demonstrating
noninferiority of MDV RSVpreF compared to SDV RSVpreF.
[ROW] Primary Safety: | 
[ROW] To describe the safety profile of
RSVpreF (MDV and SDV) as
measured by the percentage of
participants reporting local
reactions, systemic events,
AEs, and SAEs following study
intervention administration. | Overall, MDV RSVpreF was safe and well-tolerated with a safety profile comparable to SDV RSVpreF.
Reactogenicity
 Local reactions were reported at a similar frequency across the MDV and SDV groups (50.2% vs 55.1%, respectively) and
were generally mild to moderate; severe local reactions were infrequently reported in both the MDV and SDV groups (each
1.3%). The most common local reaction reported in both the MDV and SDV groups was pain at injection site (46.6% vs
52.9%, respectively). In the MDV and SDV groups, local reactions had a median onset day of Day 2 (Day 1 was the day of
vaccination), and reactions resolved with median durations of ≤3 and ≤2 days, respectively.
 Systemic events were reported at a similar frequency across the MDV and SDV groups (68.6% vs 67.4%, respectively) and
were generally mild to moderate; severe systemic events were reported by 2.7% and 1.8% of the MDV and SDV groups,
respectively. The most frequently reported systemic event in both the MDV andSDV groups was fatigue (47.5% vs 46.7%,
respectively). In the MDV and SDV groups, systemic events had a median onset day of Day 2 and Day 1 (Day 1 was the day
of vaccination), respectively, and events resolved with median durations of ≤2 days and ≤3 days,respectively.
 Medically attended events were reported infrequently across the MDV and SDV groups (each 0.4%). No immediate event,
events leading to withdrawal, or SAE were reported in either group.
end_table
